author: @TheBilly6978
text:
Just come across this video, a good demonstration but I will say keep all skin covered preferably with a pair of fire retardant overalls.
As a welder I have had some cracking sun tans on uncovered skin, normally around the neck area. If skin is left uncovered it will burn very badly.
Keep up the good work Robert

author: @John-rw9bv
text: If it's an AC welder the polarity can't make any difference, but on a DC welder it can make a big difference if you are welding anything with an oxide layer, like Aluminium or, er, dirty aluminium.

author: @JLieppinen
text: Very nice tutorial. I intent to start practicing stick welding soon and been looking for a tutorial, happy I found one here. Is there a possibility of you being part of an arc/the electricity conducting through you to the negative? What kind of safety measure one needs to prepare so you will not get electrocuted during welding? On the video it seemed there really isn't much risk as you can touch the stick while welding and even get pretty close to the negative.

author: @marcogallazzi9049
text: Wearing protection clothes in this case would be a smart thing to do, don´t wear anything flamable as you will notice you are in flames after you take your mask down. Just google some leather clothes, should not be expensive. I don´t know the tecnical name in english. Another thing is, rods are ment to be either on the positive or the negative electrode, it should be written on the package.

author: @BASEDinMaine
text:
Compliments to Robert's natural teaching style -- I think it worth note for observers that he made each sequential lesson as "dumb" and "plain simple" as possible before adding any more layers of information (this is not an insult to the student, it's just good teaching style):  from the equipment to simply making a spark to incrementally trying to be more controlled with the rod he progressed the lesson as any good teacher does by standing back and observing the student to see when the lesson can be advanced or where more subtle coaching could be offered.
It's more common and less effective to front load information that is largely irrelevant to the first necessary steps.  Bravo good sirs!

author: @johnzudans7724
text: I’m currently teaching kids at risk how to weld starting with stick welding. I like your approach, but I do not encourage holding one hand on the rod. I ask the students to support their stick hand with the free hand. I have found that steadying the stick hand with the other hand helps to strike the arc and keep the arc close to the metal and reduces the rod from sticking. Thanks for your good example. I have also found it takes only a few minutes for the welding to start and that often ladies are a bit better at this than men. Most students have to overcome the initial fear of the arc. Once that occurs, welding basics become simple.

replies.author: @Dizzy_Daveo
replies.text: @leorumley8132 If you're using scrap, make sure to remove the rust before attempting to weld otherwise the rust gets trapped between the 2 layers of metal leaving air bubbles inside the weld like an Aero chocolate bar which will make the weld weaker :)

author: @Sam3532
text: I thought in terms of safety the negative should be on the work piece so you less likely to short the live to ground through yourself

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I'm not sure it works that way mate - I though earth as ground was only appropriate if the supply also uses earth as ground - that way you had the same reference for the potential - if using a dc battery for example it doesn't ground through earth as the point of reference for the potential is the negative side of the battery not the earth.

replies.author: @Sam3532
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Im not a welder.. I should have kept my mouth shut! 😂 I took to google after reading your comment and learnt a lot! Apparently deaths from welding due to electrocution are rare; and I also learnt that most stick welders use a relatively low voltage. Though still, care should be taken when working with electricity, of course. ..Apparently more people suffer from asphyxiation when welding 🤭
Keep up the great content! And thanks for the reply 🥳

author: @jamesharris5158
text:
Fantastic video. Brought a smile to my face. One critical piece of safety information missing though... Always keep your skin covered when welding. There's a lot of UV coming off that arc and the "sunburn" you can get in a relatively short time is brutal...

There are reasons why polarity matters, but for the hobbyist sticking a few bits of random steel together, like you said, not particularly important...
